attributes.stem:
An 85-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-year history of progressive swelling, pruritus and brownish discolouration of her right shin and calf, which has worsened over the past six weeks. She has a medical history of type 2 diabetes, left mastectomy with chemotherapy and radiotherapy for breast cancer ten years ago, and a right leg femoro-popliteal deep vein thrombosis post-operatively that was treated with anticoagulation. Her medications include oral hypoglycaemic agents. She is a former smoker and drinks one glass of wine daily.

On examination, she appears well with pulse rate 89/min, blood pressure 142/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.6°C. The changes in her right leg are shown in the photograph (Image). There is some oedema, thickening and induration of the overlying skin. The skin temperature is normal and there is no tenderness. The rest of the examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Oral antibiotics and review in one week
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of chronic venous insufficiency secondary to her previous deep vein thrombosis. The brownish discolouration (haemosiderin deposition), oedema, skin thickening, and location in the gaiter region are characteristic of stasis dermatitis. The absence of warmth, acute erythema, and systemic symptoms excludes acute infection.

Elastic compression stockings are the first-line treatment for chronic venous insufficiency. They improve venous return, reduce oedema, and help prevent progression to venous ulceration. Compression therapy should be initiated after excluding significant peripheral arterial disease.

Oral antibiotics would be inappropriate as there are no signs of bacterial infection. The chronic nature of symptoms, normal skin temperature, and absence of acute erythema or systemic symptoms make cellulitis unlikely.

Intravenous antibiotics would be indicated for severe cellulitis or erysipelas, but this patient shows no signs of acute bacterial infection requiring hospital-level treatment.

Calcium dobesilate cream is a venotonic agent that may have some benefit in chronic venous disease, but it is not first-line therapy and compression stockings are more effective for managing the underlying venous hypertension.

Intermittent pneumatic compression is typically reserved for patients who cannot tolerate compression stockings or have severe venous insufficiency. It is not the initial treatment of choice for uncomplicated chronic venous insufficiency.
learningPoints: Chronic venous insufficiency presents with skin discolouration, oedema, and stasis dermatitis in the gaiter region.
Elastic compression stockings are the first-line treatment for chronic venous insufficiency.
Previous deep vein thrombosis is a significant risk factor for developing chronic venous insufficiency.
references: Sundaresan S, Migden MR, Silapunt S. Stasis Dermatitis: Pathophysiology, Evaluation, and Management. Am J Clin Dermatol. 2017 Jun;18(3):383-390. doi: 10.1007/s40257-016-0250-0. PMID: 28063094.
Agnihothri R, Shinkai K. Stasis Dermatitis. JAMA Dermatol. 2021 Dec 1;157(12):1524. doi: 10.1001/jamadermatol.2021.3475. PMID: 34643659.

De Maeseneer, Marianne G., et al. "Editor's Choice–European Society for Vascular Surgery (ESVS) 2022 Clinical Practice Guidelines on the Management of Chronic Venous Disease of the Lower Limbs." European Journal of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery 63.2 (2022): 184-267.
